coredata.dc:title: A flexible and hemostatic chitosan, polyvinyl alcohol, carbon dot nanocomposite sponge for wound dressing application
coredata.dc:creator: Shakiba-Marani, Robabeh
Ehtesabi, Hamide
coredata.dc:description: For decades, wound healing has been the focus of many researchers around the world, and their final objective is the development of effective approaches capable of accelerating the healing procedure. On the other hand, wound dressings with high flexibility, rapid hemostasis, good biocompatibility, and suitable porosity have received increasing attention. In general, skin trauma with uncontrolled bleeding is one of the main challenges in the management of clinical wounds. In the study, nanocomposite sponges of chitosan (CS)/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)/carbon dots (CDs) were successfully fabricated by using the freeze-drying method. The physicochemical properties of the prepared nanocomposite sponges were analyzed through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, porosity, water absorption, water vapor transmission rate, and mechanical tests. The results show that the obtained CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponges had high porosity, hydrophilicity, good water absorption capacity, and flexibility. In the following, in vitro cytotoxicity assays, hemolysis, and blood clotting exhibited favorable biocompatibility and nontoxicity. Besides, in vivo hemostatic potential tests on rat tail, liver, and leg injuries indicate that the sponge had high hemostatic efficiency. Taken together, the reported results indicate that the fabricated CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponges could be used as wound dressings.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Biopolymer
Wound healing

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Water absorption capacity and water vapor transmission rate (WVTR)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the process conducted to prepare CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponge.

Characterization of CDs (a) FTIR and (b) UV–Vis; (c) CS/PVA composite sponge; (d) CS/PVA/CD4 nanocomposite sponge; (e–g) SEM images of the CS/PVA composite sponge and (f,h) CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponge; (i) FTIR spectra of CS/PVA and CS/PVA/CD4 sponges.
(a) Porosity, (b) Water contact angle, (c) Water absorption, (d) Water vapor transmission rate of CS/PVA/CDs sponges.
(a) Photos of bending CS/PVA and CS/PVA/CD4, (b) tensile strength, (c) Elongation at break of the CS/PVA/CDs sponges.

(a) The whole blood clotting of various sponges; (b) Images of hemolytic activity assay of the sponges; (c) Hemolysis assays treated using different composite sponges; (d) L929 cells viability treated using various composite sponges.
Estimation of the efficacy of CD4 utilized in (a) rat tail amputation, (b) rat liver injury, and (c) rat leg artery injury models.

head.ce:title: A flexible and hemostatic chitosan, polyvinyl alcohol, carbon dot nanocomposite sponge for wound dressing application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For decades, wound healing has been the focus of many researchers around the world, and their final objective is the development of effective approaches capable of accelerating the healing procedure. On the other hand, wound dressings with high flexibility, rapid hemostasis, good biocompatibility, and suitable porosity have received increasing attention. In general, skin trauma with uncontrolled bleeding is one of the main challenges in the management of clinical wounds. In the study, nanocomposite sponges of chitosan (CS)/polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)/carbon dots (CDs) were successfully fabricated by using the freeze-drying method. The physicochemical properties of the prepared nanocomposite sponges were analyzed through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, scanning electron microscopy, porosity, water absorption, water vapor transmission rate, and mechanical tests. The results show that the obtained CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponges had high porosity, hydrophilicity, good water absorption capacity, and flexibility. In the following, in vitro cytotoxicity assays, hemolysis, and blood clotting exhibited favorable biocompatibility and nontoxicity. Besides, in vivo hemostatic potential tests on rat tail, liver, and leg injuries indicate that the sponge had high hemostatic efficiency. Taken together, the reported results indicate that the fabricated CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponges could be used as wound dressings.

ce:section.ce:para: Skin provides a vital natural barrier against the environment and exerts a variety of essential protective functions [1]. According to the definition presented by the National Cancer Institute (NCI), which is a subsidiary of the National Institutes of Health (NIH), a wound is “a break resulting from surgical incision (cut) or injury in the skin or other tissues of the body” [2]. Due to the diverse pathophysiological conditions and an increasing number of traumas, skin wounds with severe effects on people's health and lives have attracted noticeable global attention. In particular, extensive full-thickness wounds can lead to numerous problems, such as infection, bleeding, scarring, inflammation, regeneration of the hair follicles, failure to heal, and also impaired angiogenesis. Wound healing is a multifaceted and very complicated biological phenomenon comprised of inflammation, hemostasis, remodeling of the extracellular matrix (ECM), and cell proliferation [3]. At the moment, a variety of strategic techniques are commonly applied for wound treatment, which include, but are not limited to, transplantation, the use of instruments, wound dressings, and cell therapy. However, the most frequently used technique for wound treatment is applying various wound dressings in accordance with the wound depth, type, size, and location. Generally, the wound dressing is a physical barrier situated between the external environment and the wound, which is capable of protecting the wound against microorganisms and further damage and finally increases the pace of wound healing. Actually, the development of wound dressings has been aimed at affording an optimum environment for wound healing. Ideal wound dressings ought to imitate the role of the natural epithelium and serve as a barrier against damaging external factors. In order to promote the wound healing procedure, optimized wound dressings should be capable of protecting the wound against external factors, controlling bleeding, retaining moisture near the surface of the wound, and also preventing wound infection through antibacterial activity. Recently, biomaterial-based wound dressings have been extensively utilized in a variety of forms, e.g., membranes, hydrogels, sponges, and tissue engineering scaffolds. This is attributable to their non-toxicity, biocompatibility, moisture retention, easy availability, and degradability [4].

Sponges are solid, foam-like structures capable of absorbing large volumes of liquids as a result of their high porosity [5]. The benefits of employing sponges are high swelling capacity, healing of wounds without the need for scar formation, and retention of a moist atmosphere [6]. Given their porosity, swelling profile, and biodegradability, sponges can absorb large volumes of wound exudate and also maintain a moist environment at the wound site. In addition, since sponges are typically non-adhesive, they need bandages/tapes or secondary dressings to maintain them at the wound site [7].

Nanomaterials exemplify fascinating characteristics by combining their merits of miniscule size and vastly enlarged surface area, and they have a vast potential for multipurpose applications [8,9]. Due to their favorable biocompatibility, carbon nanostructures have been employed for wound healing purposes in the last decade. Because of their fair water solubility, hydrophilic nature, good cell permeability, low cytotoxicity, ease of functionalization, and good biocompatibility, carbon dots (CDs) have demonstrated hopeful potential applicability for wound dressing purposes as a category of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials [10,11]. One of the partially deacetylated products of chitin is chitosan (CS). CS is the only cationic polysaccharide that contains amino groups in nature. CS, as a widely used wound dressing, is biodegradable, biocompatible, inexpensive, moisture-retentive, and nontoxic [12]. Nonetheless, since it features poor stability in physiological mediums, poor solubility in traditional solvents, and also poor flexibility, instead of being used in pure form, it is commonly utilized with other materials [13]. Also, since CS is characterized by a mucoadhesive nature, removing CS-containing wound dressings causes pain because the dressing sticks to the wound surface most of the time and, after absorbing exudates, loses stability of its structure [14]. As a hydrophilic, non-toxic, biocompatible, and biodegradable material, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is widely used in biomedical fields, such as drug delivery, tissue engineering, and wound healing [15]. PVA is capable of improving the mechanical characteristics, including ductility and flexibility, of other polymeric materials via polymer blends and assists in keeping a moisturized medium in the vicinity of wounds [13,14].

This investigation has studied the formation of sponge NPs by employing the unique characteristics of PVA, CDs, and CS. In the first place, Okra was employed as a green and natural precursor for CD synthesis through a hydrothermal technique. The green synthesized CDs were utilized for the fabrication of CS/PVA/CD sponges via the freeze-drying technique. Through water absorption, the transfer of water vapor, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM), the physical and morphological characteristics of the nanocomposite sponges were evaluated. Additionally, in order to estimate the mechanical and physical characteristics of the nanocomposite sponges, contact angle measurements and mechanical property testing were carried out. Also, the biocompatibility of nanocomposite sponges was subjected to in vitro tests on L929 cells. In order to analyze the blood-clotting capability of the composite sponges, blood clotting was estimated in vitro. Also, in order to study the biocompatibility of the sponges, their hemolytic activity was estimated in vitro. In addition, animal experiments were carried out in vivo to investigate the effectiveness and hemostatic capability of the concerned composite sponge. According to the above results, the CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposites sponge could be a potential candidate for wound dressing purposes.

CS (viscosity 200–800 cP, 1 wt% in 1 % acetic acid at 25 °C, M. W ∼193,400 Da, degree of deacetylation 75–85 %) and PVA (MW 89,000–98,000) from Sigma-Aldrich, edible gelatin as a cream-colored powder from Halal Gelatin Pharmaceutical Processing Company (Iran), acetic acid, and glycerol (density = 1.255 at 20 °C) from Bio Basic Inc., and the Okra (Abelmoschus esculentus L.) plant's fruit from an Iranian market were purchased.

Through the hydrothermal technique and using Okra, the CDs were prepared. In summary, 2 g of the beaten Okra was dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water. The solution was then placed within an autoclave made of stainless steel coated with Teflon, and the mixture was kept in an oven at 200 °C for 24 h. A dark brown solution was acquired after cooling the mixture at ambient temperature, which was then passed through a filter paper. In order to discard the large particles, the resulting solution underwent centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for a period of 10 min. Eventually, through a PTFE syringe filter featuring a 0.22 mm pore size, the supernatant was filtered, which was then kept at 4 °C for additional usage and characterization.
For optical and chemical characterization of the CDs, UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV–visible, LAMBDA 950) and FTIR (FT-IR, Tensor 27 Bruker, Germany) were carried out.

Using a typical cross-linking technique, the composite sponge was prepared (Fig. 1 ). In sum, after adding 0.5 mL of acetic acid and 1 g of CS, the solution was stirred evenly. In addition, 1 g of PVA was dissolved in distilled water (25 mL), which was heated at 90 °C using magnetic stirring. 0.5 g of gelatin was added to the CS solution after an hour. 1 mL of glycerol was then added to the solution. After adding PVA to the solution, it was stirred for 1 h. Then 1, 2 and 4 mL CDs were added to the above solution (CS/PVA/CD1, CS/PVA/CD2, CS/PVA/CD4 respectively), which was evenly mixed using a mechanical stirrer for 30 min. Subsequently, the ropy mixed solution was transferred into plates for 24 h in order to form a hydrogel. Eventually, the obtained hydrogel was kept in a freezer dryer for 24 h at −40 °C in order to acquire the nanocomposite sponge. The obtained nanocomposite sponge was titled CS/PVA/CDs, which was then kept in a sealed bag for additional usage and characterization.

Using SEM (FESEM; Tescan Mira3, Czech Republic), all wound dressings were morphologically characterized, and the entire samples were coated with a layer of gold before experiments. Through FTIR (FT-IR, Tensor 27, Bruker, and Germany) analysis, the infrared spectrum of the sponges was measured.

Using the liquid displacement technique, the porosity of the sponges was determined. The circular shaped samples (thickness: 8.0 mm and diameter: 1.5 cm) were placed in anhydrous ethanol until reaching a saturation point. After removing the samples from the ethanol, they were gently blotted with filter paper in order to discard the excess ethanol and weighed (W2) immediately. Using the following formula, the porosity was determined:
(1)
P =
W 2 − W 1
/ ρV
In which V represents the sample volume before immersion; W2 and W1 stand for the weight of samples after and before immersion in ethanol, respectively; and ρ is the alcohol density.

Using a contact angle analyzer (Data physics Instruments, GmbH, Germany), the water contact angle (θ) was measured at room temperature. In each measurement, a drop of distilled water was placed on the surface of the sponges, and then an image was captured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water absorption capacity and water vapor transmission rate (WVTR)
ce:section.ce:para:
The capacity of water absorption of the sponges was concluded by the weight variation after the immersion of a piece of sponge into the water at room temperature until the saturation of the sponge with the liquid. The sponge was dipped in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (pH 7.4) and kept until no change was observed in its weight. The following equation was used to calculate the liquid absorption capacity (L):
(2)
L % =
m 1 − m 0
/ m 0 × 100
In which m1 (g) and m0 (g) stand for the dressing quality after and before the absorption of PBS.

The WVTR was measured in accordance with the E96-00 procedure recommended by the American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) [16]. The natural water evaporation at regular room temperature was utilized as a control, and all the experimental elements were located in the same environment. The circular shaped sponges (thickness: 8.0 mm, diameter: 1.5 cm) were hermetically situated on the mouth of a cylindrical glass bottle, which contained 10 mL of distilled water, and it was ensured that no water vapor would be lost through its mouth except through the sponge. The mass loss of water in the cup after 24 h was estimated using the following equation:
(3)
WVTR % =
Wi − Wt
/ A
g / m 2 / day
In which Wi, Wt, and A stand for the initial weight of the bottle containing water (g), the weight of the bottle containing water (g) after 24 h, and the area of the bottle mouth (m2), respectively.

By employing a texture analyzer (FTC, TPA, and American), tensile strength and elongation at break tests were conducted at room temperature in order to estimate the mechanical characteristics of the sponge.

The hemolysis ratios of samples underwent in vitro tests at various concentrations [17]. After immersion of the sample in normal saline and its incubation at 37 °C, a volume of 20 μL of erythrocyte stock dispersions was added to the sample suspensions (1 mL) and incubated for 1 h at 37 °C. Subsequently, the mixtures underwent centrifugation for 5 min at 2000 rpm/min. The supernatant absorbance at 545 nm was decided using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-1200 MAPADA, China). To eliminate the effect of the absorbance of the samples, normal saline mixed with samples and distilled water mixed with samples were set as negative control and positive control, respectively. After repeating the same experiment three times, the following equation was employed to calculate the hemolysis rate (HR, %).
(4)
HR % =
Ds − Dn
/
Dp − Dn
× 100
In which Dn, Dp, and Ds stand for the absorbance of the normal saline mixed with samples, distilled water mixed with samples, and the sample, respectively.

Blood clotting investigations were carried out in vitro. After dispersing a total volume of 100 mL of citrated whole blood over each sponge (100 mg), 20 mL of 0.2 M CaCl2 solution was added. Subsequently, the sponge was subjected to incubation at 37 °C for 30 min. Then, in order to remove the uncoagulated blood, the dish was rinsed using 25 mL of DI water. Eventually, the absorption of the uncoagulated blood solution was determined at 540 nm (abs1). As a control, a volume of 100 mL of the whole citrated blood was measured in a volume of 25 mL of DI water (abs0). Equation (-), presented below, was employed in order to estimate the blood clotting index (BCI):
(5)
BCI % =
abs 1
/
abs 0
× 100

The ultraviolet sterilized sponges were dispersed in DMEM after being cut into small pieces. L929 cells (100 μL) were inoculated in 96-well plates at a concentration of 5 × 103 cells/mL. The sponge suspensions (100 μL, 400 μg/mL) and pure PBS without adding samples were added after the sponges had been cultured for 24 h. After 24 h of incubation, MTT reagent (10 μL) was added to each well after 24 h of incubation, and each well was subsequently cultured for about 4 h. The MTT-containing solution was then swapped out for 200 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), which was then agitated for 30 min. Finally, the absorbance of the solution in the well at 570 nm was measured at 492 nm using a microplate reader (Tecan Austria GmbH Untersbergstrasse 1, A-5082, Austria) (655 nm as reference). Cell viability was determined by:
(6)
Cell viability % =
Sample
abs
492 − 655
/
Control
abs 492 − 655
× 100

In accordance with the experimental results, CS/PVA/CD4, with the optimum performance amongst CS/PVA/CDs, was chosen and utilized in the animal experiments that follow. Using rat liver injury, rat leg artery, and rat tail amputation models, the hemostatic impact of CS/PVA/CD4 was estimated. By irradiation of UV light for 1 h, the CS/PVA/CD4 slices (with a thickness of 5 mm and a diameter of 20 mm) were sterilized. Male rats weighing 200–250 g were used. The rats were anesthetized by intraperitoneal injection of 10 % chloral hydrate and fixed on the plate. All animal experiments were performed in strict accordance with National Institutes of Health (NIH) guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals and approved by the Animal Ethical Committee at Shahid Beheshti University.

Following anesthesia, the 4 cm rat tail was cut off at the tip. To ensure normal bleeding, the first drop of blood was wiped off with absorbent cotton. Next, sponges were applied to the rat's tail and treated right away. Until the bleeding stopped, the blood loss was recorded. There are six rats in each group.

Following anesthesia, the liver was exposed by shaving off the abdominal hair and opening the abdominal cavity. The KAI biopsy punch (diameter: 5 mm; depth: 1.2 mm) created a semi-ellipsoidal wound on the liver to cause bleeding. The samples were put on the bleeding site and carefully lifted to check for normal bleeding every 5 s until hemostasis was reached after normal bleeding had been confirmed. In the meantime, filter paper was placed on top of the material to absorb extra blood and measure the blood loss amount. The amount of blood lost during the operation to treat liver damage was therefore recorded. Six rats are in each group.

The left femoral artery was exposed using a scalpel and scissors in the femoral artery injury mode. The main femoral artery was entirely severed, resulting in a major arterial bleeding wound. Samples were applied to the site right away, and blood loss was recorded. There are six rats in each group.

FTIR was used to verify the successful syntheses of CDs. In Fig. 2a, the functional groups present in CDs underwent an FTIR analysis. As the FTIR spectrum indicates, the broad vibrational peaks at 3396 cm−1 are attributable to the –OH, while the peak at 2900 cm−1 is attributable to CH stretching vibration. The CC bending vibrations of benzene rings and the stretching vibrational band of –C=O were detected at 1400 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1, respectively. The vibrational peak detected at 1030 cm−1 indicated the C–O–C stretching. The above observation necessitates that synthesized fluorescent CDs contain functional carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [18]. The UV visible (UV–Vis) absorption was analyzed to further approve the successful synthesis of CDs in order to decide their optical characteristics. A peak detected at 210 nm for the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs solution in Fig. 2b is attributable to the п-п* transition as a result of CC conjugated bands. The peak tail extends up to the visible region. Our prepared CDs emitted a sharp blue light under UV radiation, whereas they are brown when subjected to daylight. These transitions are due to the functional groups present on the CDs' surface.

As seen in Figs. 5, 2c and d, CS/PVA composite sponges are white, whereas CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponges are light brown, demonstrating a uniform dispersion of CDs. Using SEM, the morphology of CS/PVA/CDs and CS/PVA composite sponges were examined, the results of which are presented in Fig. 2(e–h). The changes that occurred in surficial morphology before and after the incorporation of CDs into the CS/PVA composite sponge are clearly shown in the SEM images. The increased smoothness of the CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponge with the increase in CDs can be attributed to the characteristics of CDs, which serve as fillers and thus contribute to the smoothness of the sponge surface [19]. According to the FTIR graph, the representative peaks of the hydroxyl group showed overlap with amine group stretching vibrations around 3255 cm−1, followed by CH stretching at 2930 cm−1, found in the main scaffold. The CO and CO stretching vibrations and also primary amine peaks were detected at 1255 and 1400 cm−1, respectively. Additionally, the peak at 1030 cm−1 is specific to the CN stretching vibration (Fig. 2i) [20]. In general, the attendance of a broad peak about 3300–3500 cm−1 at a frequency lower than that of the CS spectrum implies the creation of hydrogen bonds between OH from PVA and OH or NH2 from CS.

To evaluate the water-holding capacity of the nanocomposite sponges, their porosity was investigated. Fig. 3a indicates that by adding more CDs and increasing their concentration, the sponges' porosity increased from 76 % to 149 %. The porosity of CS/PVA was considerably lower than that of CS/PVA/CDs, which is likely attributable to the addition of CDs, which can improve the material's water content and hydrophilicity. This increase in porosity was matched with SEM pictures of the nanocomposite, which showed a porous 3D network structure with a relatively smooth pore wall. The higher porosity of wound dressing contributes to the absorbance of wound exudates and the prevention of wound infections. In addition, the presence of larger porosities contributed to the improved transfer of oxygen and nutrients to the cells attached to the wound dressings [21,22].

One of the crucial issues for wound healing is the hydrophilicity of wound dressing sponges. Water contact angle (θ) is a suitable parameter to determine the hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of all dressing surfaces, namely, sponges [23]. The contact angle between water droplets and sponges was determined, the results of which are presented in Fig. 3b. Based on the results, the contact angle reduces with increasing CD concentration, from 123° in the CS/PVA composite sponge to 68.67° in the CS/PVA/CD4 sponge. The presence of hydrophilic groups on the surfaces of CDs changes the wettability of nanocomposite sponges and increases their hydrophilicity. Thus, one can conclude that CDs improved the hydrophilicity of the CS/PVA composite sponge [24,25].

The capability of the wound dressing in the retention and absorption of water is a crucial characteristic in order to estimate the efficacy of wound clearing [26]. The evaluation of the water absorption capability of the sponges is presented in Fig. 3c. Compared to the water absorption capability of the CS/PVA composite sponge (1362.06 %), the CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponges showed higher water absorption potential of 2103.125 to 2893 %, which is ascribable to CDs serving as a cross-linking site for the promotion of the interactions between PVA and CS [27]. In general, increased hydrophilicity can increase the absorption of wound exudates and maintain a moist environment around the wound bed.

Water vapor permeation is a critical parameter to test the suitability of a scaffold for external purposes, including wound dressing. If excessive amounts of water are lost through the material, it will cause the wound to dry, which may result in a delayed process of wound healing. The moisture permeability of a wound dressing material should not be too high, as this could result in severe moisture loss, nor too low, as this could lead to exudate leakage [28]. The observed WVTRs for a variety of sponges were 1012, 920, 933, and 880 g/m2/day for CS/PVA, CS/PVA/CD1, CS/PVA/CD2, and CS/PVA/CD4, respectively (Fig. 3d). This result is ascribable to the porosity and the pore size. Also, this may be due to the hydrophilicity of the CDs, which improves the tendency of water vapor to transport as hydrophilicity increases. In general, depending on the severity of skin injuries, the required WVTR is 279–5318 g/m2/day, and the WVTR of CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponges is capable of meeting the permeability demands of the wound dressings [29].

The mechanical characteristics of sponges are key features in wound dressing. Since wound dressing comes in close contact with the skin surface, its mechanical strength must be tested to control the comfort of the patient when its material is close to the skin surface [30]. The mechanical characteristics of the whole sponges were determined as depicted in Fig. 4 a–c. Elongation at the break and tensile strength of sponges were determined. As Fig. 4b shows, for both sample and control, the tensile strength was within the 0.001–0.01 MPa range. As Fig. 4c indicates, elongation at break reflects the flexibility of composites that ranged between 20 % and 30 %. To be ideal, a wound dressing bandage should be flexible and strong. According to the conducted studies, if CS is present in composite sponges, it can decrease their tensile strength, whereas it improves the elongation at the break point and thus enhances their flexibility. With the addition of CDs, the flexibility of sponge is enhanced. As a result, one can hold the sponges on all surfaces without being broken [31].

Hemolysis is a reliable and very simple technique to estimate the blood compatibility of wound dressings. Based on the guidelines issued by the national safety standards, the rate of hemolysis for the hemostatic materials utilized in organisms cannot exceed 5 % [30]. In order to estimate the hemolysis ratio of CS/PVA/CDs and CS/PVA composite sponges, a hemolysis test was carried out, the results of which are presented in Fig. 5 (b–c). The addition of CDs resulted in a reduced hemolysis rate of the sponges. The hemolysis rate for CS/PVA/CDs is clearly lower than 5 %, which meets the requirements of the national safety standards. The lower hemolysis rate results in better blood compatibility and lower RBC capability. Given the above results, the nanocomposite sponge is a material featuring suitable blood compatibility [32].

One of the traditional techniques used to estimate the coagulation ability of hemostatic materials is whole blood coagulation. Lower values of the Blood Clotting Index (BCI) enhance the coagulation speed [33]. The blood clotting test was carried out in order to estimate the hemostatic potential of the CS/PVA/CDs and CS/PVA composite sponges. The in vitro blood clotting potential of the composite sponges is shown in Fig. 5a. According to the results, the blood clotting capability of the CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponges surpassed that of the CS/PVA composite sponges. The improved hemostatic potential of the CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponge is attributable to their morphological characteristics, microstructure, and chemical composition. In the first place, cationic CS is capable of absorbing negatively charged red blood cells, plasma proteins, and blood fibrinogen. In addition, the highly porous CS dressings are capable of improving the absorption potential. By the incorporation of CDs, the blood-clotting potential of the CS dressing is improved further. This is because CDs are capable of affecting the intrinsic blood coagulation pathway and denaturing the anticoagulant proteins [34].

One of the primary requirements for wound dressing purposes is biocompatibility. Using the MTT assay, the viability of L929 murine fibroblasts cells in contact with sponges CS/PVA/CDs and CS/PVA was quantified indirectly and compared to the controls. Then, the comparison results were presented in Fig. 5d. The positive controls showed the highest absorption after 24, 48, and 72 h of incubation. At the beginning and after an incubation of 24 h, the cell viability for CS/PVA/CD1, CS/PVA/CD2 and CS/PVA/CD4 nanocomposite sponges decreased in comparison with CS/PVA. Nonetheless, for the CS/PVA/CD4 nanocomposite sponge, the cell viability was enhanced noticeably by about 93 % after 48 h. During 72 h, although the cell viability treated with CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponge declines as the CDs content of the dressing increases, it is still significantly higher than 70 %. Cell viability results indicate that both CS/PVA and CS/PVA/CDs sponges show no cytotoxicity toward the L929 cells. The excellent biocompatibility of CS and the sponges' porous structure probably account for the sponges' high cell vitality. According to ISO-10993-5-2009 criteria, a cytotoxic chemical is one in which less than 70 % of cells exposed to it for 24 hour survive [35,36].

At present, the most widely used technique of hemostasis for deep traumas or penetrating wounds is filling the hemostatic gauze into the wound and applying external pressure in order to decrease the rapid blood loss. Nonetheless, this technique is faced with limitations, and the probability of death of the injured individuals is still high. By employing the rat liver trauma model, rat leg artery trauma model, and rat tail amputation model, the hemostatic effectiveness and capability of the nanocomposite sponge CS/PVA/CS4 were estimated, the results of which are presented in Fig. 6 (a, b, and c). As the images in Fig. 6(a–c) show, compared to the gauze application, the amount of blood loss in the CS/PVA/CD4 and CS/PVA groups was lower. The blood loss recorded for the use of the CS/PVA/CD4 nanocomposite sponge on rat liver trauma, rat leg artery trauma, and rat tail amputation was 3.8 g, 4.6 g, and 1.3 g, respectively, while that of the gauze was 1.5 g, 1 g, and 2.5 g (Fig. 6(a–c)). The nanocomposite sponge showed superior potential in the reduction of blood loss compared to the CS/PVA composite sponge and gauze. The main aspects of the hemostatic mechanism of the composite hemostatic sponge are as follows. In the first place, after placing the hemostatic sponge into the wound, some of the blood's water content will be absorbed quickly so that the blood becomes more concentrated, leading to thrombosis formation based on its strong water absorption potential. Secondly, the CS on the composite hemostatic sponge surface is capable of promoting the aggregation of red blood cells and platelets for thrombus formation. In the meantime, the CS is capable of stimulating the coagulation reaction of the body and further accelerating the thrombus formation. Thirdly, after absorbing the blood, the compressed hemostatic sponge is expanded quickly, and by relying on its capability of fast-paced shape recovery after water absorption, it forms a certain compression force on the wound. It is capable of compressing blood vessels in order to block further blood outflow and achieve rapid hemostasis. In addition, according to previous reports, CDs are capable of interacting with the proteins found in the blood and forming a complex with iron ions found in the plasma to reduce the coagulation time further and achieve rapid hemostasis [37].

In conclusion, we designed the CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponges by using the freeze-drying method. The CDs used in making the nanocomposite sponges were prepared from a new green source called “Okra”. The CDs were directly synthesized through a simple hydrothermal method. Using CDs in different amounts could control many properties of the prepared sponge, including porosity, hydrophilic/hydrophobic properties, water absorbance capacity, WVTR, and mechanical properties. The prepared nanocomposite sponges showed high porosity, hydrophilicity, and better water absorption, which is favorable for absorbing wound exudate. A suitable WVTR of nanocomposite sponges was obtained, which can provide an ideal moist environment for wound healing. SEM images showed that the loading of CDs changes the pore size of the sponges. The mechanical tests showed that the flexibility of CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponges was improved. With the increase of CDs incorporation, the cytotoxicity, hemostatic and blood-clotting capabilities increased, indicating the excellent cytocompatibility and hemocompatibility of the CS/PVA/CDs nanocomposite sponges. In three animal injury models, it was demonstrated that the CS/PVA/CD4 nanocomposite sponge had a rapid hemostatic effect compared with gauze and the CS/PVA group. To sum up, we indicated that the nanocomposite sponges, especially CS/PVA/CD4, had great potential to be applied as a wound dressing.

Robabeh Shakiba-Marani: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Hamide Ehtesabi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
